coredata.dc:title: Ternary catalysts based on amino-functionalized carbon quantum dots, graphitic carbon nitride nanosheets and cobalt complex for efficient H2 evolution under visible light irradiation
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Yunxiao
Zhang, Wei-De
coredata.dc:description: Preparation of earth-abundant and noble-metal-free transition metal complexes serving as photocatalytic hydrogen evolution co-catalysts is of great challenge and significance. Here, ternary photocatalysts Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CQDs/CN by amide bonds coupling Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2 [dcbpy = (4, 4′-dicarboxy-2, 2′-bipyridine)] with amino-functionalized carbon quantum dots (CQDs) or graphitic carbon nitride nanosheets (CN) were developed for efficient solar-to-hydrogen conversion. The amide bonds accelerate the combination of Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2 and CQDs/CN and improve the dispersion of Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2 on CN framework, thus leading to enhanced migration of charge carriers and absorption of visible light. In addition, the optimum photocatalytic hydrogen evolution activity of Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CQDs/CN catalyst reaches 295.9 μmol h−1 g−1 with excellent stability after four cycling tests under visible light irradiation. The hydrogen evolution activity of 3Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CQDs/CN catalyst reaches 75.02 μmol h−1 g−1 under monochromatic light irradiation (λ = 450 nm) and the apparent quantum efficiency reaches 14.11%, whereas pure CN is inactive under the same condition. The amide bond between cobalt-based complex and CN plays an important role for excellent photocatalytic performance. More importantly, the composite catalysts give a new insight for designing noble-metal-free co-catalysts with excellent photocatalytic activity and high stability towards solar-to-hydrogen conversion.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photocatalyst
Graphitic carbon nitride
CQDs
Cobalt complex
Amide bond
Hydrogen evolution

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of amino-functionalized carbon quantum dots (CQDs)

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21773074 , 21273080 ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Representation of synthetic process of Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CQDs/CN catalysts. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
SEM images of (A, B, C) CN, (D, E, F) 3Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CQDs/CN catalyst.

TEM images of (A) CN, (B–D) 3Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CQDs/CN catalyst. (E) EDX spectrum and the elemental composition (inset), (F–J) elemental mappings of 3Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CQDs/CN catalyst. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
XRD patterns of CN and Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CQDs/CN catalysts. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) XPS survey spectra, high-resolution XPS spectra of (B) C 1s region, (C) N 1s region of CN and 3Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CQDs/CN catalyst. High-resolution XPS spectra of (D) O 1s, (E) Co 2p and (F) S 2p of 3Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CQDs/CN catalyst. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
(A and B) FTIR spectra of CN and Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CQDs/CN catalysts. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) Photocatalytic H2 evolution rate of Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CQDs/CN catalysts under visible light irradiation (λ > 420 nm). (B) Photocatalytic H2 evolution performance of Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CQDs/CN catalysts. (C) Stability tests of 3Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CQDs/CN catalyst for 4 cycles in 16 h. (D) Wavelength dependence of the hydrogen evolution rates of CN and 3Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CQDs/CN catalyst. (E) Photocatalytic H2 evolution rate of Co(dcbpy)2Cl2/CN, Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CN and Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CQDs/CN catalysts. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) UV–vis diffuse reflection spectra of CN and Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CQDs/CN catalysts. (B) Photoluminescence spectra of CN, CQDs/CN, and Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CQDs/CN catalysts. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) Transient photocurrent response under visible light irradiation (λ > 420 nm), (B) electrochemical impedance spectroscopy in the dark and (C) surface photovoltage spectra of CN, 3Co(dcbpy)2Cl2/CN, 3Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CN and 3Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CQDs/CN catalysts. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Linear sweep voltammograms (LSV) of CN, 3Co(dcbpy)2Cl2/CN, 3Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CN and 3Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CQDs/CN catalysts. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Electronic structure of polymeric trimmer models and optimized HOMO and LUMO energy levels of CN trimmer and Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CN trimmer. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

The color variation of the suspension of 3Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CQDs/CN catalyst (50 mg photocatalysts, 10 mL TEOA and 90 mL DI water) during the process of photocatalytic hydrogen evolution under visible light irradiation (λ > 420 nm). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Schematic diagram of photogenerated electrons and holes transfer process of Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CQDs/CN catalysts under visible light irradiation. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Ternary catalysts based on amino-functionalized carbon quantum dots, graphitic carbon nitride nanosheets and cobalt complex for efficient H2 evolution under visible light irradiation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Functional Molecular Engineering of Guangdong Province, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, South China University of Technology, 381 Wushan Road, Guangzhou, 510640, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Functional Molecular Engineering of Guangdong Province
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Preparation of earth-abundant and noble-metal-free transition metal complexes serving as photocatalytic hydrogen evolution co-catalysts is of great challenge and significance. Here, ternary photocatalysts Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CQDs/CN by amide bonds coupling Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2 [dcbpy = (4, 4′-dicarboxy-2, 2′-bipyridine)] with amino-functionalized carbon quantum dots (CQDs) or graphitic carbon nitride nanosheets (CN) were developed for efficient solar-to-hydrogen conversion. The amide bonds accelerate the combination of Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2 and CQDs/CN and improve the dispersion of Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2 on CN framework, thus leading to enhanced migration of charge carriers and absorption of visible light. In addition, the optimum photocatalytic hydrogen evolution activity of Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CQDs/CN catalyst reaches 295.9 μmol h−1 g−1 with excellent stability after four cycling tests under visible light irradiation. The hydrogen evolution activity of 3Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CQDs/CN catalyst reaches 75.02 μmol h−1 g−1 under monochromatic light irradiation (λ = 450 nm) and the apparent quantum efficiency reaches 14.11%, whereas pure CN is inactive under the same condition. The amide bond between cobalt-based complex and CN plays an important role for excellent photocatalytic performance. More importantly, the composite catalysts give a new insight for designing noble-metal-free co-catalysts with excellent photocatalytic activity and high stability towards solar-to-hydrogen conversion.

Ternary photocatalysts Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CQDs/CN were developed by amide bonds coupling Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2 [dcbpy = (4, 4′-dicarboxy-2, 2′-bipyridine)] with amino-functionalized carbon quantum dots (CQDs) or graphitic carbon nitride nanosheets (CN) for efficient solar-to-hydrogen conversion. This study demonstrates a simple way to design noble-metal-free co-catalysts with excellent photocatalytic activity and high stability towards solar-to-hydrogen conversion.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Photocatalytic water splitting has been considered as a promising technology to produce hydrogen and store solar-converted energy to address current global energy crisis and environmental pollution for almost half a century [1–3]. Various photoactive materials, such as TiO2 [4], CdS [5], WO3 [6], and ZnS [7], have been investigated constantly in order to find the optimal solution. To date, growing number of efforts have been attempted to develop an efficiency-high, cost-effective, and environment-friendly photocatalyst.

Notably, graphitic carbon nitride (CN), a two-dimensional conjugated polymer, has emerged as a novel metal-free photocatalyst under visible light [8]. In view of the limited utilization of visible light and high recombination of photo-generated carriers for graphitic carbon nitride, various strategies, such as doping by metal or non-metal elements [9,10], constructing heterojunctions with other semiconductors [11,12], morphology-controlled nanotubes and nanospheres [13,14], have been developed to enhance the photocatalytic activity for hydrogen evolution. Nevertheless, precious-metal-based co-catalysts are essential in order to get superior photocatalytic activity. Recently, incorporation of earth-abundant and low-cost co-catalysts, such as MoS2 [15], NiS [16,17], metal phosphide [18], nickel complexes [19–22], and cobalt complexes [23–25], has displayed excellent performance and superior stability as substitutes for noble-metal based catalysts. However, the inherent weakness of the utilized low-cost co-catalysts is their poor distribution on graphitic carbon nitride framework and comparatively low photocatalytic activity. Some metal complexes, such as Ru(II) mononuclear complexes and binuclear complexes, exhibited high photocatalytic activity considering carboxylic or phosphonic acid as anchoring groups to bind with semiconductors [26–34]. The Ru(II) complexes were mainly employed in CO2 reduction from an aqueous or organic solution containing sacrificial electron donors, but modification of the metal complexes on graphitic carbon nitride framework with robust anchoring groups for efficient photocatalytic hydrogen evolution activity is rare.

Herein, we report a facile novel artificial photosynthetic system binding graphitic carbon nitride with cobalt-based complex Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2 as a co-catalyst and light absorbing material to promote photocatalytic performance in aqueous solution under visible light irradiation. The robust binding is ascribed to the formation of amide bond, which is developed by amide condensation reaction of the partial condensation amino in CN and carboxyl in Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2. Meanwhile, amino-functionalized carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are considered to enhance the mobility of photo-generated electrons. On the other hand, the reaction of amido in CQDs and carboxyl in Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2 generates amide bond. In photocatalytic process, Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2 accepts electrons from the conduction band of graphitic carbon nitride directly or through CQDs. The robust binding greatly improves the distribution of cobalt complexes on graphitic carbon nitride framework and promotes hydrogen evolution activity of the composite catalysts.

Urea (99%) was obtained from Guangdong Guanghua Sci-Tech Co. Ltd. (Guangzhou, China), while 4, 4′-dicarboxyl-2, 2′-bipyridine (H2dcbpy) was adopted from Aladdin Industrial Corporation (Shanghai, China). Cobalt (II) chloride hexahydrate (CoCl2·6H2O) was acquired from Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory (Tianjin, China). Potassium thiocyanate (KSCN) was gained from Guangzhou Chemical Reagent Factory (Guangzhou, China). Chitosan was attained from Shanghai Boao biotechnology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF) and acetic acid were purchased from Jiangsu Qiangsheng functional chemical Co. Ltd. (Jiangsu, China). Triethanolamine (85%) was bought from Tianjin Fuyu Fine Chemical Co. Ltd. (Tianjin, China). All chemicals were of reagent grade and used without further purification.
The synthetic process of photocatalysts is illustrated in Fig. 1 and Fig. S1 (Supplementary Information).

Graphitic carbon nitride (CN) was synthesized by heating 5.0 g urea in a porcelain boat under Ar atmosphere flowing at 550 °C for 4 h in a tube furnace and then cooled down to room temperature. The obtained samples were collected and labeled as CN.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of amino-functionalized carbon quantum dots (CQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: CQDs were prepared according to the method described in a previous report [35]. Briefly, 2 g chitosan was dissolved in 18 mL 2% acetic acid solution and then the mixture was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave to continue the hydrothermal treatment lasting for 12 h at 180 °C. After cooling down to ambient temperature, the obtained dark-brown solution was centrifuged and the brown supernatant was labeled as CQDs solution. The yield of CQDs is 7.8% and the solubility of the CQDs reaches 8.7 mg mL−1.

Typically, 0.1 g CN was dissolved in 20 mL DMF and sonicated for 30 min. Certain amounts (0, 1, 2, 3, 4 mg) of H2dcbpy were dissolved in 10 mL DMF and then transferred into the above suspension. After stirring for 30 min, certain amounts (0, 0.5, 1, 1.5, 2 mg) of CoCl2·6H2O were also dissolved in 10 mL DMF and then added into the suspension [36]. After stirring overnight, the products were obtained by filtering the solution and drying at 80 °C overnight. The samples were labeled as xCo(dcbpy)2Cl2/CN (x = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4).

Preparation of Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CN catalysts was similar to that of Co(dcbpy)2Cl2/CN. Additionally, after adding H2dcbpy and CoCl2·6H2O into the suspension of CN and stirring overnight, certain amounts (0, 8, 16, 24, 32 mg) of KSCN were dissolved in 10 mL water and 30 mL DMF, and then transferred into the suspension. After being heated at 100 °C for 4 h followed by cooling down to room temperature [37,38], the products were obtained by filtering and drying. The samples were labeled as xCo(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CN (x = 0, 1, 2, 3 and 4).

0.1 g CN and n mL (n = 0.01, 0.05, 0.10, 0.30 and 0.50) CQDs were dissolved in 20 mL DMF and sonicated for 30 min. After adding 2 mg H2dcbpy and 1 mg CoCl2·6H2O into the above suspension and stirring overnight, 16 mg KSCN was dissolved in 10 mL water and 30 mL DMF and transferred into the above suspension and then heated at 100 °C for 4 h. The samples were labeled on the basis of the amounts of CQDs and named as Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/xCQDs/CN (x = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5).

xCo(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CQDs/CN were prepared followed a similar process as xCo(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CN. Firstly, 0.1 g CN and 0.10 mL CQDs were dissolved in 20 mL DMF and sonicated for 30 min. Secondly, certain amounts of H2dcbpy (0, 1, 2, 3, 4 mg) dissolved in 10 mL DMF were added into the suspension. Thirdly, corresponding amounts of CoCl2·6H2O (0, 0.5, 1, 1.5, 2 mg) dissolved in 10 mL DMF and KSCN (0, 8, 16, 24, 32 mg) dissolved in a mixed solution of 10 mL H2O and 30 mL DMF were consecutively transferred into the suspension. Lastly, the samples were attained by filtering and drying, and were marked as xCo(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CQDs/CN (x = 0, 1, 2, 3 and 4) based on the amounts of H2 (dcbpy), CoCl2·6H2O and KSCN (without the complex, that is x = 0, the sample was remarked as CQDs/CN). The detailed preparation conditions for different catalysts are shown in Table S1. The theoretical mass fractions of Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2, CQDs, CN, and cobalt in the xCo(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CQDs/CN catalysts are shown in Table S2.

DFT calculations were conducted using Guassian09 program to simulate the HOMO and LUMO energy levels of CN and Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CN catalysts. The B3LYP functions and 6–31 G (d, p) basis set were utilized for all calculations. To further simulate the experimental results qualitatively, two trimer units were used as the calculation model.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analyses were carried out on a Thermo ESCALab250-X1 electron spectrometer. All binding energy was calibrated by the C 1s peak of the surface adventitious carbon located at 284.8 eV. The morphologies of the as-prepared samples were observed by field emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Merlin, Zeiss) at an acceleration voltage of 5 kV and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100F) at an acceleration voltage of 300 kV. UV–vis diffuse reflection spectra were recorded on a Hitachi U-3010 spectrometer over a scan range of 200–800 nm using BaSO4 as a reference. The crystalline structures of the as-prepared samples were characterized by X-ray powder diffraction (XRD, Bruker GADDS, Cu Kα radiation) in the 2θ range of 5–90° with intervals of 0.02°. An IR Affiniy-1 spectrometer was used to record the Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra over a scan range of 400–4000 cm−1 with KBr pellet as the matrix, while a F-4500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (315 nm excitation) was used to survey the photoluminescence (PL) measurement at room temperature. Atomic absorption spectrometer (AAS, Z-2000) was used to determine the content of cobalt in the as-prepared samples. The steady-state surface photovoltage spectra (SPV) were acquired on a self-made instrument [21].

Photoelectrochemical analyses were carried out on an electrochemical workstation (CHI 660C, Chenhua, Shanghai), equipped with a conventional three-electrode system using KCl-saturated Ag/AgCl electrode as a reference electrode and a Pt plate as a counter electrode. 10 mg of the as-prepared samples and 1 mg ethyl cellulose were dispersed in 1 mL ethanol, then the suspension was uniformly casted onto a fluorine-doped-tin oxide (FTO) glass as a working electrode, which was dried at 100 °C for 1 h before use. 0.5 M Na2SO4 solution served as the electrolyte and the incident light was gained from a 300 W Xenon lamp (PLS-300/300UV) under visible light region (λ > 420 nm). The on/off photocurrent intensity responses were measured at −0.1 V vs. an Ag/AgCl electrode. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) plots were performed on the corresponding open circuit potential at the frequency ranging from 106 to 10−2 Hz in 0.01 M K3Fe(CN)6/K4Fe(CN)6 (1:1) solution in the dark. The linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) was scanned from −0.58 to −1.38 V (vs. Ag/AgCl, pH = 6.5).

Photocatalytic activity of hydrogen evolution was performed in a top-irradiation reaction vessel (Pyrex glass) connected to a closed glass gas circulation system (Labsolar III AG, Beijing Perfectlight Technology Co. Ltd., China). Typically, 50 mg catalyst was well-dispersed into 100 mL aqueous solution containing 10 vol% triethanolamine as a sacrificial agent under stirring. The system was vacuumed while the suspension was evacuated several times to remove the air before photocatalytic reaction. The reaction solution was irradiated by a 300 W Xe lamp (PLS-SXE 300/300UV, Beijing Perfectlight Technology Co. Ltd, China) with an ultraviolet cut-off filter (λ > 420 nm). The average light intensity was adjusted to 100 mW cm−2. The evolved H2 was determined by a gas chromatography (GC 7806, Beijing Shiweipuxin Analytical Instruments Co. Ltd, China) with a thermal conductive detector (TCD) and a 5A molecular sieve column using N2 as a carry gas. The apparent quantum efficiency (AQE) was tested under the same conditions except for substituting the ultraviolet cut-off filter for the monochromatic light with a band-pass filter (400, 450, 500, 550, and 600 nm). The optical power intensity of the irradiation at 450 nm was measured to be 2.04 mW cm−2. The number of incident photos (N) was calculated according to equation (1) as follows. AQE was defined by equation (2).
(1)

=
E λ
h c

p h o t o s

The morphologies and microstructures of CN and 3Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CQDs/CN catalyst were characterized by SEM and TEM. As shown in Fig. 2 A–C, CN exhibits typical thin nanosheets morphology. For 3Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CQDs/CN (Fig. 2D–F), the SEM images reveal the similar nanosheets with CN, indicating that the incorporation of CQDs and Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2 are closely associated with the CN framework. Because of the small size of CQDs, it is hard to identify them in 3Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CQDs/CN. The SEM images of 3Co(dcbpy)2Cl2/CN and 3Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CN catalysts are shown in Fig. S2. All the catalysts exhibit similar nanostructures, indicating that the modification of cobalt-based complexes or CQDs does not change the CN framework. For the TEM images, CN (Fig. 3 A) and 3Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CQDs/CN (Fig. 3B) exhibit similar sheet-like outlook. In Fig. 3C, high-magnification TEM image of 3Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CQDs/CN manifests that CQDs are uniformly dispersed on CN framework (the yellow rings). The corresponding elemental mappings and X-ray (EDX) spectrum of 3Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CQDs/CN are depicted in Fig. 3D–J, illustrating that C, N, O, Co and S atoms are homogeneously distributed. The mass fraction of Co (0.19 wt.%) in 3Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CQDs/CN is different with the theoretical mass fraction of Co (0.34 wt.%, Tables S1–S2), indicating that portion of Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2 dissolved in DMF solution rather than binding with CN or CQDs. The result is consistent with the obtained blue solution after synthesis of Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CQDs/CN catalysts. Co, O and S atoms are derived from Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2, while the high distribution of the three atoms verifies the effective binding of CN, CQDs and Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2 rather than physically mixed.

XRD patterns of pristine CN and Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CQDs/CN catalysts are shown in Fig. 4 . Two characteristic peaks located at 13.1° and 27.6°, corresponding to (100) and (002) diffraction planes of CN respectively, are reflected on the samples [39,40], in which the peak located at 13.1° is related to the inter-planar packing of tris-s-triazine and 27.6° is related to the interlayer d-spacing. The diffraction peaks located at 18.7°, 29.8°, and 30.7° appeared in 3Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CQDs/CN and 4Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CQDs/CN catalysts, which may be ascribed to the increased loading contents of Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2. Obviously, the intensity of the characterized diffraction peak located at 27.6° for CN decreases with the increase of Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2 contents, meaning that the introduction of Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2 intercalates the CN congeries and restricts the graphitic stacking of CN [40,41]. The small nano-particles of CQDs exhibit no signals in the XRD patterns, which is consistent with SEM analyses.

XPS spectra were used to analyze the surface species and chemical states of CN and 3Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CQDs/CN catalyst (Fig. 5 ). XPS survey spectra reveal that the coexistence of C, N and O elements in CN, while C, N, O, Co and S elements in 3Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CQDs/CN (Fig. 5A). As shown in Table S3, small amounts of Co (0.19 at%) and S (0.44 at%) co-exist in 3Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CQDs/CN and the atom ratio is close to 1:2, which is consistent with the component of Co and S in Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2 complex. The increased C/N atom ratio from 0.76 to 1.15 is attributed to the introduction of CQDs and Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2. In Fig. 5B, for CN, two distinct peaks located at 284.8 and 288.3 eV are ascribed to surface adventitious carbon (C=C) and sp2-hybridized carbon (N−C=N), respectively [42]. For 3Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CQDs/CN catalyst, the distinct peak for sp2-hybridized carbon located at 288.6 eV shifts to higher binding energy than that of pure CN, which indicates the decreased electron density around the C atoms [43,44]. It is also obvious that other three characteristic peaks at 285.7, 292.7 and 295.5 eV appeared, corresponding to the amides C (C=O) [45], the shake-up peak [46] and the satellite of C 1s [47], respectively. The shake-up peak is a characteristic of conjugated systems or aromatic groups [48], indicating the existence of CQDs. Upon introduction of CQDs and Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2, the relative ratio of C=C rose (Table S4). Furthermore, the peaks shifted to higher binding energy, corresponding to the changes of chemical states [43,44]. High-resolution N 1s spectra are shown in Fig. 5C. Four constituent peaks located at 398.7, 399.4, 400.9 and 404.7 eV are ascribed to sp2-hybridized aromatic nitrogen (C−N=C, N2C), tertiary nitrogen (N−(C)3, N3C), quaternary nitrogen (C−NHx, N1C) and π excitations, respectively [49]. N 1s spectra for 3Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CQDs/CN catalyst are similar to those of CN. Notably, the peaks of N2C and N3C shift to higher binding energy, corresponding to the decreased electron density around N atoms in CN framework in 3Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CQDs/CN, which is ascribed to the incorporation of CQDs and Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2 [43]. Compared to pure CN, the electron density around C and N atoms of 3Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CQDs/CN catalyst decreased, because Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2 attracts electrons from CN or CQDs/CN after their combination [43,44]. As shown in Table S5, compared to CN, the relative ratio of quaternary N for 3Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CQDs/CN catalyst decreased, which is attributed to the formation of amide bond between CN and Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2. In Fig. 5D, the peak located at 531.8 and 533.1 eV are assigned to C=O and −OH, respectively, indicating the formation of amide bond [50]. The binding energy of Co 2p3/2 in 3Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CQDs/CN is located at 781.6 eV, corresponding to the Co (II) state in Co−Nx (Fig. 5E). Compared to the binding energy of metallic Co (ca. 779 eV) and Co oxide (ca. 780 eV), the chemical shift of 781.6 eV is higher. The main reason is that the high electronegativity of N (3.04) caused the higher shift compared to that of Co (1.88) and O (2.55), indicating the presence of Co⋅⋅⋅N bond rather than CoOx or other composites [23]. As shown in Fig. 5F, the chemical shift of 161.8 and 162.9 eV discloses the existence of S in the NCS ligands [51]. According to the result of XPS, Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CQDs/CN catalyst was successfully synthesized and the amide bonds were formed by amide condensation reaction.

FTIR spectra were recorded to study the functional groups of CN and Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CQDs/CN catalysts (Fig. 6 ). As shown in Fig. 6A, all the samples exhibit similar absorption signals at 3000–3500, 1200–1700 and 810 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching vibration modes of −NH or O−H groups, the stretching vibration modes of aromatic C−N heterocycles and the breathing vibration of the heptazine rings [52], respectively. The characteristic signals prove that the introduction of CQDs and Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2 does not affect its essential structure of CN. Compared to pure CN, upon increasing incorporation of CQDs and Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2, intensity of the broad absorption peaks at 3000-3400 cm−1 becomes weaker, indicating the decreased amounts of amino groups, which is attributed to the formation of amide bonds for CN or CQDs with Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2. The results and the XPS analysis are perfectly coherent. For Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CQDs/CN catalysts, the increased contents of Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2 lead to two characteristic peaks located at 2062 and 3114 cm−1, corresponding to NCS groups [53,54] and the stretching vibration modes of C=C−H bonds [55], which is attributed to the characteristic stretching modes of Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2. The partial absorption peaks at 900-2000 cm−1 are shown in Fig. 6B. Compared to pure CN, several absorption peaks at 1719, 1604, 1367 and 1290 cm−1 are assigned to the extension vibration of C=O bonds [56], acidamide II [57], −NHCO− groups [58] and the extension vibration of C−N on amide bond [56], further indicating that Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2 was successfully bound with CN and CQDs by amide bond. According to the FTIR spectra shown in Fig. S3, it is also proved that Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CQDs/CN catalysts were successfully synthesized rather than just physically mixed by CoCl2, KSCN, H2dcbpy, and CN.

To verify the binding effect of Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2 and CQDs on the photocatalytic performance of the samples, controlled experiments were conducted to illustrate the significance of every component for the synthesis of Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CQDs/CN catalysts. The photocatalytic H2 production of Co(dcbpy)2Cl2/CN, Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CN and Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/xCQDs/CN catalysts are shown in Fig. S4A–S4F. Without Co(dcbpy)2Cl2 or Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2, CN or CN/CQDs exhibits negligible photocatalytic activity. It is an evidence that Co(dcbpy)2Cl2 or Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2 serves as a co-catalyst to promote H2 production. As shown in Fig. S4A, 3Co(dcbpy)2Cl2/CN catalyst reaches a peak hydrogen evolution rate of 195.1 μmol h−1 g−1. With the introduction of KSCN, the peak hydrogen evolution rate of 3Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CN catalyst is 214.8 μmol h−1 g−1 (Fig. S4B). To investigate the effect of CQDs on photocatalytic performance, the hydrogen evolution rate of catalysts with various contents of CQDs in the Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CQDs/CN catalysts is shown in Fig. S4C. After being modified with CQDs, hydrogen evolution activity increases significantly, while the peak hydrogen evolution rate is 256.8 μmol h−1 g−1 and the volume of CQDs solution is 0.1 mL. According to the controlled experiments, Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2 and CQDs are beneficial for photocatalytic hydrogen evolution. The influence of binding with CQDs and different contents of Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2 on their photocatalytic hydrogen evolution performance of Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CQDs/CN catalysts was further investigated. As shown in Fig. 7 A, upon increasing content of Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2, the hydrogen evolution rate is raised, especially for 3Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CQDs/CN catalyst, which reaches a peak hydrogen evolution rate of 295.9 μmol h−1 g−1. The higher contents of Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2 lead to reduction of hydrogen production, which is ascribed to that the excess Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2 would act as new recombination sites for photogenerated electrons and holes [29]. After 4 h test, the hydrogen evolution rate of CN, CQDs/CN, and Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CQDs/CN catalysts are still stable (Fig. 7B). It is noted that both CN and CQDs/CN are inactive in the reaction. As shown in Fig. 7C, the hydrogen evolution activity of 3Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CQDs/CN catalyst presents slight decrease after 4 cycles in 16 h, suggesting its high photocatalytic stability. Fig. 7D shows the corresponding UV–vis spectra and the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution of 3Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CQDs/CN catalyst under monochromatic light irradiation (λ = 400, 450, 500, 550 and 600 nm). The hydrogen evolution rate is relevant to the wavelength of light. Corresponding to the UV–vis spectra, 3Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CQDs/CN catalyst still exhibits photocatalytic hydrogen evolution activity under 550 and 600 nm visible light irradiation, which is ascribed to the absorbance of Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2. Meanwhile, the hydrogen evolution rate is 75.02 μmol h−1 g−1 at 450 nm and the AQE is calculated to be 14.11%. The photocatalytic hydrogen evolution rate of Co(dcbpy)2Cl2/CN, Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CN and Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CQDs/CN catalysts are summarized and shown in Fig. 7E. It is obvious that the introduction of Co(dcbpy)2Cl2, Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2 and CQDs significantly promotes the photocatalytic activity of CN.

UV–vis diffuse reflection spectra and photoluminescence (PL) were measured to investigate the optical properties of pure CN and Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CQDs/CN catalysts in Fig. 8 . Fig. 8A displays a rapid increase in the absorption edge located at 455 nm, which is ascribed to the inherent absorption of CN with a band gap of ca. 2.7 eV [59]. Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CQDs/CN catalysts exhibit a broad absorption in the range of 450–700 nm, which is attributed to Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2 [29]. Obviously, Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2 also promotes light absorption. With the increased amounts of Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2, it is obvious that the peak intensity becomes higher, indicating more binding amounts of Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2 in the Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CQDs/CN catalysts. The broad absorption is corresponding to the high photocatalytic activity of 3Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CQDs/CN under monochromatic light irradiation (Fig. 7D). UV–vis diffuse reflection spectra of KSCN, CoCl2, H2dcbpy, CN, and Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CQDs/CN are shown in Fig. S5. The different absorption peaks confirm the existence of cobalt complex and the binding-bond in the hybrid materials, which are in line with FTIR analyses. As shown in Fig. 8B, PL spectra were recorded to disclose the recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes. The obvious PL quenching of the peaks located at around 450 nm in Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CQDs/CN catalysts indicate the restriction of recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes as the binding amounts of Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2 increased [22]. Under visible light irradiation, electrons and holes were generated and transferred to the surface of CN, and then migrated to Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2, benefitting from the amide bonds in the Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CQDs/CN catalysts. Meanwhile, compared to pure CN, the lower PL intensity of CQDs/CN indicates that the CQDs also promoted the migration of photogenerated electrons. As a result, the heterogeneous photocatalysis could be facilitated by amide bond for effective charge transformation in the Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CQDs/CN catalysts.

To further study the efficient separation and migration of the charge carriers for the as-synthesized catalysts, the on/off photocurrent intensity responses, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) plots and photovoltage spectra were measured and shown in Fig. 9 . As shown in Fig. 9A, the photocurrent intensity of the samples exhibits cathodic response on each on/off light turn. With the incorporation of Co(dcbpy)2Cl2, CQDs and Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2, the optimized samples display increased photocurrent response. Notably, 3Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CQDs/CN catalyst exhibits the highest photocurrent response than pure CN, 3Co(dcbpy)2Cl2/CN and 3Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CN, revealing that modification of CQDs and Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2 effectively enhances the separation and migration of charge carriers [40]. The EIS Nyquist plots of CN, 3Co(dcbpy)2Cl2/CN, 3Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CN and 3Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CQDs/CN catalysts are shown in Fig. 9B. The optimized samples exhibit smaller radius than pure CN, indicating the lower resistance and higher mobility of the modified photocatalyst [40], especially for 3Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CQDs/CN. The greater migratory efficiency of charge carriers leads to the remarkable photocatalytic activity of Co(dcbpy)2Cl2/CN, Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CN and Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CQDs/CN catalysts. As shown in Fig. 9C, all of the samples exhibit positive photovoltage responses in the range of 340–410 nm. Compared to pure CN, the photovoltage response intensity of 3Co(dcbpy)2Cl2/CN, 3Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CN, and 3Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CQDs/CN catalysts improved remarkably. The higher photovoltage intensity indicates the higher efficiency of charge separation [17,18].

Electrochemical liner sweep voltammetry (LSV) was performed to reveal the electrochemical properties of the CN and hybridized materials. The LSV curves of CN, 3Co(dcbpy)2Cl2/CN, 3Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CN and 3Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CQDs/CN catalysts are shown in Fig. 10 . Notably, all the hybridized materials exhibit decreased onset potentials and enhanced cathodic current compared to pure CN, especially for 3Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CQDs/CN. The results are consistent with the hydrogen generation performance, indicating that Co(dcbpy)2Cl2 and Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2 work as co-catalysts during photocatalytic reactions [52,60,61].

To further prove the role of cobalt-based complexes, DFT calculations were performed and the result is shown in Fig. 11 . For CN, the HOMO mostly stems from the combination of nitrogen p
z orbitals, while the LUMO mainly comes from the C−N bond orbitals [62]. Compared to CN, the incorporation of Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CN could obviously change the HOMO and LUMO levels of Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CN catalyst. In detailed, the HOMO and LUMO of the Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CN trimmer mainly locate on the Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2 segment. Binding Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2 with CN can effectively promote the delocalization of π-conjugated electrons which is beneficial for charge transfer, resulting in high photocatalytic performance [40]. Interestingly, during the photocatalytic reaction, the color of the suspension gradually becomes greener under visible light irradiation (Fig. 12 ), indicating that redox reactions occurred for Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2. Notably, once the reaction system was terminated and the light was turned off, the green melted into pale yellow. The results reveal that the redox reactions are reversible. According to the above results, the possible photocatalytic mechanism is illustrated in Fig. 13 . Electrons and holes are generated in pure CN under irradiation of visible light. The holes in VB of CN are quenched by TEOA, while the electrons in CB of CN are able to reduce H+ to H2 in two ways. First, the photoexcited electrons transfer from the CB of CN to the Cobalt (II) of Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2 (blue arrow) to form the reduced Cobalt(I) species, a highly reactive intermediate. The introduction of proton yields the Co(III)H species. Further addition of protons to the Co(III)H species produces dihydrogen, and re-generates the starting Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2 [19,22,63,64]. Second, the electrons in CB of CN transfer to CQDs (green arrow) and then to the Cobalt (II) of Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2, supplying the reduced Cobalt(I) species. The following steps are the same as the first one. In sum, the electrons are devoted to the reduction of H+ to H2.

Ternary noble-metal-free Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CQDs/CN catalysts were successfully synthesized by compounding CN or CQDs with Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2 through amide bond, which effectively promoted the dispersion and combination of Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2 on the CN framework or CQDs rather than just a physical mixture of the aforementioned elements. The chemical binding of the active components further promotes the transportation of interface charge carriers. Furthermore, the incorporation of CQDs and Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2 significantly facilitated the absorption of visible light. In addition, the optimum photocatalytic hydrogen evolution activity of Co(dcbpy)2(NCS)2/CQDs/CN catalysts reaches 295.9 μmol h−1 g−1 with excellent stability after four cycling tests. More importantly, the system offers a new insight for designing high performance catalysts with transition metal complex as a stable earth-abundant and noble-metal-free co-catalyst for highly photocatalytic hydrogen activity.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21773074, 21273080).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2019.01.052.
